author: @crohkorthreetoes3821
text: Something most people miss on the Tesla homopolar motor. He mentions that a second rubber belt can be attached around the drive rods. Well, If you really think about that The rubber belt is a high voltage circuit, while the copper belt is a high amperage circuit. This oft overlooked comment in the patent greatly effects the efficiency of this device.

author: @Khwartz
text:
Dear Robert, first: thanks again for your amazing work and sharing! :D

About not wanting to go in discussions about ratio of net power if an electrical input by mean of an electric motor, I understand you but it is only because you were not projecting a self loop. Cause if the output power if higher enough than the input, you should be able to loop it and then, no more problem of calculations needed, no discussion needed: that fact proves by itself the overunity COP (I remember anyone that COP is not the "physical efficiency", no law of physic needs to be broken to have an overunity COP while a overunity "physical efficiency" would.).

replies.author: @Khwartz
replies.text:
So,  lets say:

2 V * 5 mA = 10 mW

10 mW * 500 sheets = 5 W

500 sheets * (20 cm large * 30 cm high * 0.2 cm per layer including the space)
= 500 sheets * (0.20 m large * 0.30 m high * 0.002 m per layer including the space)
= 0.06 m3

5 W / 0.06 m3 = 83 W/m3 For Free.

1 kWh of residential solar electricity costs 0.12 $.

1 kWh / 83 W/m3 * 0.12 $/kWh = 144 $, 

which would be the limite cost of financial interest for 1 m3 of SWAG in this configuration.

500 sheets * (0.20 m large * 0.30 m high) = 30 m²

144 $ / 30 m² = 4.8 $/m²

which would be the limite cost of financial interest for 1 m2 of SWAG in this configuration,

which loos to me LARGELY FAISABLE with your process!

Don't You think so?

Dearest Robert.

Ref: http://solarcellcentral.com/cost_page.html

author: @MrCajunchaos1
text: In regards to a self contained unit (absent an ocean) do you think it would be possible to use a capillary siphon.  Imagine if you will a box containing the arrangement of plates (gills).  The water level in the box rests just above the gills.  Place a flow chamber at the bottom of the box.  The chamber end opposite the gills would be lined with .3 mm capillary tubes in the shape of walking sticks.  At the diameter described water can travel in excess of 100 mm above level.  The downward side of the capillary tubes would just touch a plate that is angled back toward the gills and allow flow back to and over top of the gills.  Just an idea maybe it could be of some use.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it's a good idea - i will think about how to incorporate it

author: @charlietaylor6227
text:
I searched the comments and did not see anything about Kepler's buoy... so sorry if I missed something already suggested.  All those years ago Kepler created a wave pump by simply placing a floating buoy in the sea with a pipe, a flat plate was attached to a smaller pipe with a sliding seal between and 2 flow valves.  As the buoy bobbed (coupled to the surface) the plate resisted movement (resistively coupled to still water below the waves) so the seal moved up and down in the pipe.  The 2 flow valves turned that into a pump.  It seems to me that if the SWAG was simply on a stiff shaft on the buoy it would be puled up and down through the relatively still water by the buoy movement.  No "moving parts" relatively speaking!  A matrix of these buoys bobbing up and down could generate an enormous amount of amps!  For stability, how about a tube (PVC pipe?) with the upper float and lower SWAG sitting over a rod driven into the sea bottom in shallow water.  Ah, simplicity!
Alas... no ocean in Denver, CO USA
PS... also making "graphene sheets by rubbing graphite into a plastic sheet similar to your other video.  It seems this would also work for the SWAG - no?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i'll try the rubbing idea but i love the buoy idea - thanks for that

replies.author: @charlietaylor6227
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith Along with the Kepler buoy another of Tesla's favorites was the water wall generator.  Basically you build a short walled enclosure out in the bay or a large floating barge with a "Skirt" wall.  The wall is 2/3 the wave height above average. (6 ft sea, 1 foot wall).  The upper third of the waves wash over the wall and fill the enclosure.  You open a restricted outlet and use the low head to turn a turbine... Not much head!  But, perfect for putting water over a SWAG!

author: @FredGandt
text:
I'm personally gratified to see a box full of sheets and a tapered collection tube ;-)
*But* I still think there's a strong possibility that a dramatically turbulent flow might produce better results and beg you to test it.
My thinking is this (in a nutch):
If as the speed of flow increases, so does the power output, it appears that the _energy_ output is relative to the _energy_ input (although "duh" could be muttered, it wouldn't be nice). So the more energetic the input is (movement across the sheet), the more is produced.
Why turbulent?
Simply since fluids tend toward it, so it easy to produce (allow), and at the scale of the actual effect (atomic) a smooth flow is nonsense - there simple isn't such a thing.
So why not take advantage of the natural order of fluid dynamics (it's far harder to stop fluids crashing and smashing, than to make them) and at an atomic scale, the flow would be (if measured or even measurable) greatly increased.
_i.e._ where _n_ mps smooth is _n_ mps at an atomic scale, we might see _n_ mps turbulent being _n_ ² or greater at the atomic scale (due to stuff smashing around).
Consider how much damage is done to solid brass propellers by sea water, and due to no more than turbulence. A high energy system.

Do I make sense?

There's always the other possibility though (and perhaps you know this already?), that the smoother the flow the better. That would be a damn shame, since a fast flow that's also smooth is far less simple to achieve, and the limits far easier to fall.foul of.

Please paint a washboard and stand on a table etc. ;-)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I absolutely agree with you mate - but you know me - simple first - i'm a simple guy lol

replies.author: @FredGandt
replies.text:
Simple often equates to efficient :-)

Love the serendipity of the _perfect_ ink BTW.
Like getting a pat on the back from existence when stuff just works :-)

replies.author: @thagrit
replies.text: You still have to consider the structural strength of the package you are employing. If you make the water turbulent to increase power output you may destroy your collector. Also what happens when the sea adds it's own turbulence to yours such as coastal rough seas.

replies.author: @FredGandt

author: @deslomeslager
text:
Hi Rob

Good idea. The minute I see this I start calculating. Open voltage? Zero point something. Amps? 4 Amps. Wattage? P = U * I = ? But do not let this stop the development and testing.
PS: if you apply a voltage to such a sheet, they get warm? (hint). Why the hint? There are no diodes (yet). You would need to prevent the voltage from going into parallel plates. (just thinking out loud, that's how I do things). I compare this as with solar panels. If one panel is covered, it will drain down the power (a bit). With many plates and an 'average' resistance I think the same problem might arise. Perhaps it is best to go for a high(er) voltage. The average current will be depending on the worst generating plate, but voltages will add up, and using a dc to dc convertor from 50 Volt to 12 Volt (f.e.) can be done in 85% or better conversion rate. Blah blah. Think tank . ..

Could you please perform another test? If you have time, and just for the idea of it. Please try and put a test sheet into any salt solution and put it in the sonar. (I hope it does not do anything bad to the used ink, as I understand it has an effect on it). My idea is that if the water is 'shaking' violently, there might be something happening. And I do know the sonar uses a lot of power, but it is just the idea to test it. Suppose we put a big A4 swag box into the sonar and find something odd?

Deslo.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: interesting ideas mate

author: @ldanielrosa
text:
I'm refreshed by your optimism and I'm glad you keep one foot firmly planted while you're playing in the lab.  And for that I envy you.  Thank you for being a citizen scientist.  Sadly, I'm one of those armchair theorists.  I'll try to keep it relevant.

It took me a while to connect the dots, but this hydroelectric power implementation seems not so dissimilar to electrostatic vs. electromagnetic speakers.  Okay perhaps that wasn't a good analogy, but by changing the method of energy extraction you change the perspective of requirements and limitations.

I'm curious about the possible work density.  With low or uncontrollable water currents it may be difficult to power large items, but I can see immediate use in an anchored buoy.

This is fascinating to me, and I have so many questions.  Besides the issues of grit and silt gumming up the works, would anything become non-passively involved?  How would plankton or algae interact with this stuff?  I wonder if Sally Le Page would be interested in this project, from the biology side.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: The bet way to answer a lot of your questions is just to build the prototype and test it. You can discuss, theorize and guess as much as you like and it will often take a hell of a lot to do that but the answers will be quicker and more true if a simple prototype is built and tested
